coredata.dc:title: Efficient carbon dots/NiFe-layered double hydroxide/BiVO4 photoanodes for photoelectrochemical water splitting
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Xiaowei
Xiao, Xin
Cao, Minglei
Bu, Yi
Wang, Chuanqing
Wang, Mingkui
Shen, Yan
coredata.dc:description: Modification of semiconductor photoanodes with oxygen evolution catalyst (OEC) is an effective approach for improving photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting efficiency. In the configuration, how to increase the activity of OEC is crucial to further improve PEC performance. Herein, a ternary photoanode system was designed to enhance PEC efficiency of photoelectrodes through introducing carbon dots (CDs), NiFe-layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH) nanosheets on BiVO4 particles. Systematic research shows that NiFe-LDH serves as an OEC which accelerates oxygen evolution kinetics, while the introduction of CDs can further reduce charge transfer resistance and overpotential for oxygen evolution. Under the synergistic effect of NiFe-LDH and CDs, the photocurrent and incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of the resulting CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode is improved significantly than those of the NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 electrode. Consequently, such a ternary heterostructure could be an alternative way to further enhance PEC water splitting performance.
coredata.dcterms:subject: BiVO4
Carbon dots
NiFe-layered double hydroxide
Photoanodes
Photoelectrochemical water splitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of NiFe layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH) on BiVO4 electrodes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported from the NSFC Major International (Regional) Joint Research Project NSFC-SNSF ( 51661135023 ), NSFC ( 21673091 ), the 973 Program of China ( 2014CB643506 ), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (HUST: 2016YXMS031 ), the Director Fund of the WNLO , and the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry ( SKLEAC201607 ). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Centre of HUST and the Center of Micro-Fabrication and Characterization of WNLO for the sample characterization. Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the synthesis of CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 (a), SEM pictures of BiVO4 (b) and CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 (c), EDX mapping of CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 (d).
TEM images (a), XRD patterns (b) of the samples.

(a) UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra, (b) LSV curves, (c) calculated photoconversion efficiency, (d) IPCEs measured at an applied voltage of 1.23 V, (e) EIS measured at 1.23 V under illumination and (f) polarization curves for the samples of BiVO4, CDs/BiVO4, NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 and CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanodes, respectively.

Light harvesting efficiencies (a), the AM 1.5 G electron flux as a function of wavelength (b), charge separation efficiency (c) and charge injection efficiency (d) of the BiVO4, CDsBiVO4, NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 and CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanodes.
(a) O 1 s, (b) Bi 4f, (c) Ni 2p, (d) Fe 2p and (e) C 1 s XPS spectra of the samples. (f) Schematic energy level and charge-transfer pathways in the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode.

head.ce:title: Efficient carbon dots/NiFe-layered double hydroxide/BiVO4 photoanodes for photoelectrochemical water splitting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Wuhan National Laboratory for Optoelectronics, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Luoyu Road 1037, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modification of semiconductor photoanodes with oxygen evolution catalyst (OEC) is an effective approach for improving photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting efficiency. In the configuration, how to increase the activity of OEC is crucial to further improve PEC performance. Herein, a ternary photoanode system was designed to enhance PEC efficiency of photoelectrodes through introducing carbon dots (CDs), NiFe-layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH) nanosheets on BiVO4 particles. Systematic research shows that NiFe-LDH serves as an OEC which accelerates oxygen evolution kinetics, while the introduction of CDs can further reduce charge transfer resistance and overpotential for oxygen evolution. Under the synergistic effect of NiFe-LDH and CDs, the photocurrent and incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of the resulting CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode is improved significantly than those of the NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 electrode. Consequently, such a ternary heterostructure could be an alternative way to further enhance PEC water splitting performance.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-driven photoelectrochemical (PEC) water splitting into hydrogen gas and oxygen gas has attracted considerable interest because it is a promising approach to address the environmental crises and energy shortage issues [1,2]. The greatest challenge in this system is to accelerate the sluggish water oxidation reaction occurred at photoanode/electrolyte interface, which is a multistep, four-proton and four-electron process [3]. Modification of photoanode with oxygen evolution catalysts (OECs) has been demonstrated to be one of the most effective routes to improve interfacial reaction kinetics and therefore increase the charge injection efficiency and photocurrent [3,4]. Among various OECs used in PEC system, NiFe layered double hydroxides (NiFe-LDH) has been considered as one of the most efficient OECs owing to its intrinsic layered structure. The structure benefits the fast diffusion of reagents and resultant and makes the sluggish proton-coupled electron transfer process less stressful tocarry out. Therefore, the PEC performance of photoanode modified with NiFe-LDH has been greatly improved. For instance, Choi et al. reported a nanoporous BiVO4 film with FeOOH/NiOOH as a OEC to achieve a photocurrent density of 4.4 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V versus RHE. Moreover, a superior photocurrent density of 1.74 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V versus RHE has been reported on a TiO2 nanorod arrays modified with NiFe-LDH and reduced graphite oxide [5,6]. However, the charge injection efficiency of composites is still in a lower level, especially in low bias voltage range [6]. Furthermore, NiFe-LDH’s performance is not fully developed because of the disadvantages in its poor electrical conductivity [7]. Hence, the issue, i.e., how to further improves the charge injection efficiency remains open question for photoelectrodes in PEC system.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new kind of nanocarbon, have attracted considerable interest due to their special physicochemical characteristics [8–10]. Specifically, its superior conductivity, small grain size (about 5 nm), fast electron transfer, and electron storage properties make it easy to compose them with other photocatalysts, thus further improving the catalytic performance of the raw materials [7]. Prior researches have demonstrated that CDs-based composites such as CDs/BiVO4, CDs/TiO2, and CDs/WO3 have showed enhanced photoelectrocatalytic properties [11–14]. Based on the above research work, the introduction of CDs may improve the electrical conductivity and the electrocatalytic activity for water oxidation of NiFe-LDH, and thus further improving the PEC performance. However, CDs/NiFe-LDH-based composites as a highly effective OEC for PEC water splitting, as far asweknow, have not been reported.

Herein, as a typical presentation, BiVO4 is selected as an example to investigate the synergetic effect of NiFe-LDH and CDs on the PEC performance of BiVO4 photoanode. It turned out that the photocurrent of the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photanode is about 1.7 times as high as that of the NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 electrode. Furthermore, the roles of CDs and NiFe-LDH in composites were discussed in detail. Under the synergistic effect of NiFe-LDH and CDs, the oxygen evolution kinetics could be further improved in the ternary CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode. Therefore, the CDs/NiFe-LDH-based ternary heterostructure designed in our article turns out to be a valid route to improve PEC performance of photoanode.

A 40 mM Bi(NO3)3 solution was prepared by dissolving Bi(NO3)3·5H2O in 50 mL of a 0.4 M KI solution after its pH was adjusted to 1.7 by adding HNO3 [5,15]. This solution was blended with 0.02 L of purealcohol containing 0.23 M p-benzoquinone. A typical three-electrode system was employed for electro-deposition. A fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) acts as working electrode, an Ag/AgCl (filled with saturated KCl) serves as reference electrode, and a twisted platinum wires as counter electrode. An electrochemical workstation (CHI-750D, Shanghai Chenhua, China) was used for sample preparation and performance test. Electrochemical deposition was performed at −0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl for 5 min. 25 μL of a dimethyl sulfoxide solution containing 800 mM VO(acac)2 was placed on the surface of BiOI electrode (1 × 1 cm) and then the electrode was heated in a muffle furnace at 450 °C (ramping rate = 2 °C/min) for 2 h to convert BiOI to BiVO4. Excess V2O5 at the surface of BiVO4 was removed by immersing the electrode in 1 M NaOH for 0.5 h with slow stirring. Finally, the resulted BiVO4 electrodes were soaked with DI water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NiFe layered double hydroxide (NiFe-LDH) on BiVO4 electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: NiFe-LDH was deposited on BiVO4 electrodes by a two-step electrochemical deposition technique [16] using a Pt CE, and an Ag/AgCl (filled with saturated KCl) RE in an undivided cell. The NiFe-LDH was deposited at −0.5 V in sequence in 0.05 M (NH4)2Fe(SO4)2 and 0.05 mM Ni(NO3)2 aqueous solution. Then, the electrode was soaked with DI water for a fewminutes and then dried at room temperature. The optimized deposition time of NiFe-LDH has been determined to be 300 s. The solution was purged with nitrogen gas for 0.5 h before the experiment.

The graphite rod was used as both anode and cathode with a distance of 7.5 cm. The ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm−1, 600 mL) serves as electrolyte. 40 V DC was applied to the two graphite rod. After 120 h continuous stirring, a dark-yellow solution appeared in the electrolyzer. The solution was centrifuged at 20 000 rpm for 25 min to remove the insoluble graphite particles. Finally, the resulting solution is hydrosoluble CDs. The NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanodes were soaked into 25 mL CDs solution for 2 h, 4 h and 6 h. The resulting CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 electrodes were immersed with DI water and dried at 80 °C for 6 h.

The morphology and element contents of the resulting films were determined by field-emission scanning electron microscopy equipped with energy dispersion X-ray spectrometer (EDX) (FESEM, Nova Nano SEM 450, Netherlands). The UV–vis absorption spectra were achieved from a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimdzu, Japan) at room temperature. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was carried out on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD-600 W XPS instrument. The binding energy was referenced to the C 1 s level at 284.8 eV from exotic carbon. The PEC properties were measured with a standard three-electrode configuration by an electrochemical workstation (CHI-750D, Shanghai Chenhua, China) with 0.5 M PBS (pH = 7) solution as electrolyte. The resulting films, twisted platinum wires and Ag/AgCl electrode (filled with saturated KCl at 20 °C) were used as the working, counter and reference electrodes, respectively. Nitrogen was purged into the electrolyte for 0.5 h to remove O2 before testing. A 300 W xenon lamp (Newport, model 69911, USA) along with an AM 1.5 G filter was employed as the light source (100 mW cm−2). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) was carried out with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in the dark and light illumination. The potentials versus Ag/AgCl were converted to those versus RHE according to the Nernst equation (ERHE = EAg/AgCl + 0.059pH + 0.197). The incident photon conversion efficiency (IPCE) was measured under irradiation from light of a different wavelength generated by monochromatic filters. The IPCE values were evaluated based on the following formula [18]
(1)
IPCE =
1240 I
λ
P
light
× 100 %
where λ and Plight are the wavelength (nm) and the intensity (µW cm−2) of the incident light, and I is the photocurrent density (µA cm−2). The electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) were recorded in the dark and under light at 1.23 V versus RHE in the frequency region of 1–105 Hz with AC voltage of 5 mV. Gas chromatography (GC-2020, Trustworthy Instrument, TCD, N2 carrier) was used to guarantee the obtained photocurrent was due to water splitting. The photocurrents vs. time curves were recorded at a constant potential of 1.23 V vs. RHE in 0.5 M PBS (pH = 7) solution. Before the reactions, the cell was purged with N2 and closed off. The Faradic efficiency was evaluated as the ratio 4F
n
O
2
/Qtot, where F is the Faraday constant,
n
O
2
is the number of moles of O2, Qtot is the total charge.

Fig. 1 a demonstrates the schematic diagram of the preparation process of the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoelectrode. Above all, BiVO4 particles, 100–500 nm in diameter, were grown on FTO (Fig. 1b). Subsequently, NiFe-LDH nanosheets were evenly deposited on the BiVO4 particles followed by a dry process in air for a strong adhesion. The thickness of NiFe-LDH is about 10–20 nm, and the size is about 70–200 nm (Fig. S1). Finally, the obtained NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 was soaked in CDs solution for 4 h and dried in a vacuum oven at 80 °C for 8 h to obtain the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 composite with hierarchical heterostructure (Fig. 1c). The experimental details are provided in the supporting information. The element distribution and composition are further characterized by EDX. The results show the homogeneous distribution of CDs and NiFe-LDH on the BiVO4 particles (Fig. 1d). The content of CDs, determined by the average value of six points at different area of thesurface, is about 8.3% (as presented in Table S1). The cross-sectional SEM image and the corresponding linear EDX element distributions data of CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 show that the NiFe-LDH and CDs mainlydistribute on the surface of the compound, the content of NiFe-LDH and CDs is significantly reduced insidethe structures. That is, the NiFe-LDH and CDs cannot penetrate completely into the porous structure of BiVO4 (Fig. S2)

TEM measurement further reveals the morphology of the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 (Fig. 2 a). TEM images exhibit that CDs are uniformly dispersed on the surface of NiFe-LDH nanosheets (the inset of Fig. 2a). The average size of CDs is about 7 nm. The HRTEM image of CDs shows that the interplanar spacing of around 0.321 nm corresponds to the (002) crystallographic planes of graphitic carbon, which is strong evidence for the formation of CDs (Fig. S1). As a control sample, pristine BiVO4 and CDs/BiVO4 and NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 were also synthesized. The componentof the resulting product was analysed by XRD. The diffraction peaks of the pure BiVO4 are belonging to monoclinic BiVO4 (JCPDS No. 14-0688) (Fig. 2b). No other reflection appears after it composited with NiFe-LDH or/and CDs, the absence of the characteristic reflections is mainly due to its low content (Fig. 2b). The existence of NiFe-LDH is further confirmed by XPS results in the following pages.

In order to obtain the optimal performance of the composites, experimental factors are reviewed. The influence of the electrodeposition time of NiFe-LDH on the PEC performance of the NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 was investigated. The BiVO4 deposited with NiFe-LDH was denoted as NiFe-LDHx/BiVO4, where x is the time of electrodeposition. LSV measurements for these NiFe-LDHx/BiVO4 photoanodes show that the photocurrent successively increases from x = 100 s to x = 300 s, and then decreases with the further enhancement of the time to 400 s (Fig. S3). It is found that the PEC performance can be tuned by changing the electrodeposition time of NiFe-LDH onto BiVO4. With a moderate NiFe-LDH electrodeposition time (NiFe-LDH300s/BiVO4), NiFe-LDH nanosheets are evenly deposited on the surface of BiVO4 particles (Fig. S4). Therefore, the NiFe-LDH300s/BiVO4 with the maximum photocurrent is selected for subsequent research.

The role of the load of CDs was also examined. The BiVO4 with different amount of CDs (denoted as x-CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4, where x is soak time) was prepared by soaking NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 into CDs aqueous solution with different time. It is found that the PEC performance can be tuned by the soak time of NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 electrode in CDs solution (Fig. S8). The 4 h-CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 electrode (immersed into CDs solution for 4 h) exhibits the highest photocurrent density (CDs content of ∼8.3 wt.% decided by EDX) (Fig. 1d).To survey optical properties, the light absorption curves of BiVO4, CDs/BiVO4, NiFe-LDH/BiVO4, and CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 were measured by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. The pristine BiVO4 displays a strong absorption at the wavelength range of 300–500 nm (Fig. 3 a), After combination with CDs and NiFe-LDH, the absorption intensity of the composites increases because of the absorption of CDs and NiFe-LDH, but the band edge of the samples has no obvious change. The band gap was calculated by extending a line from the maximum slope of the Tauc plot (
α hv
versus hν) to the axis [19]. The band gap of 2.48 eV was observed for the four samples. Besides, the enhanced absorption (>500 nm) doesn’t mean an expansion of absorption. The relationship between photocurrent and absorption spectra will be analyzed in IPCE tests.

To further investigate the photoresponse performance, PEC measurements were performed using BiVO4, CDs/BiVO4, NiFe-LDH/BiVO4, and CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 as photoanodes in 0.5 M PBS solution (pH = 7). The results in dark for all films only show a small background current density at μA cm−2 scale. The pure BiVO4 shows a comparatively low photocurrent (0.79 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V) within entire voltage range (Fig. 3b). For the NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode, we can see a remarkably enhanced photocurrent density (1.93 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V) and a slightly negative shift in oneset potential. In contrast, the CDs/BiVO4 presents a slight increase in photocurrent (1.49 mA cm−2 at 1.23 V) and a parallel onset potential compared with the pure BiVO4. As for as the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4, an onset potential with significantly negative shift (−0.11 V) is manifested compared to pristine BiVO4. Compared with the literature, the onset potential falls is moderate (Table S2). The photocurrent density can reach up to of 2.84 mA cm−2 (1.23 VRHE), which is the maximum photocurrent among all the photoelectrodes. These results show that the ternary CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 is more efficient for water oxidation than the others. Besides, the photo-conversion efficiency was evaluated on the basis of the LSV curve (Fig. 3c). The CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 electrode displays a highest conversion efficiency of 0.58% at 0.82 V, which is obviously superior to that of NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 (0.34%, 0.91 V), CDs/BiVO4 (0.17%, 0.99 V) and pristine BiVO4 (0.09%, 0.96 V). The PEC performance comparisons clearly demonstrate a synergistic effect of CDs and NiFe-LDH in the ternary CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 on BiVO4. Furthermore, the IPCE is characterized to investigate the connection between the photoelectrocatalytic properties and light absorption (Fig. 3d). The highest IPCE values are observed at 380 nm, which are 19.17%, 22.97%, 24.59%, and 40.94% for BiVO4, CDs/BiVO4, NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 and CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4, respectively. The obviously increased IPCE value of the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 further verifies the synergistic effect of NiFe-LDH and CDs to the PEC properties. Moreover, the IPCE values is almost zero at the wavelength of more than 500 nm, revealing that only BiVO4 is the photoresponse material, while the absorption at visible light range caused by NiFe-LDH and CDs cannot bring a photocurrent for water oxidation.

To investigate the charge transport kinetics of these photoanodes, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were performed. An equivalent circuit model (the inset of Fig. 3e) was employed to analyze the EIS data, in which Rs is the resistance of the electrolyte, CPE is the capacitance phase element, Rct represents the charge-transfer resistance at the film/electrolyte interface, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3e, the charge transfer resistance (equal to the semicircle diameter in nyquist diagram) follows a descending order as: BiVO4 (652 Ω) > CDs/BiVO4 (558 Ω) > NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 (440 Ω) > CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 (336 Ω). Obviously, with the incorporation of CDs and NiFe-LDH in sequence, the resistance is gradually reduce. Finally, the ternary CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode displays the smallest charge transfer resistance among these four samples, that is, the ternary CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode possesses the fastest charge transport kinetics.

To verify the role of CDs in the OER process, we further performed polarization measurements on the samples. Fig. 3f shows the polarization curves for these films at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1 in 0.5 M PBS solution (pH = 7). As compared to pristine BiVO4, the BiVO4 modified with CDs and/or NiFe-LDH show a differentdegreeof decrease in onset potentials and exhibit higher current. Concretely, the onset potentials show the following trendency of BiVO4 > CDs/BiVO4 > NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 > CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4. Obviously, CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 electrode displays lowest overpotential. This indicates that the introduction of CDs can further reduce the anodic overpotential for oxygen evolution. Consequently the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode possesses a significantly enhanced OER activity. In addition, the overpotential of the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 for electrochemical oxidation reaction is reduced by about 0.35 V, which is significantly greater than the onset potential shift in the photocurrent curve (0.11 V). This may be attributed to poor charge carrier mobility at the photoelectrode/OEC interface [6]. The overpotential of pure CDs and NiFe-LDH also be measured for comparision. The results presented in Fig. S5 show that the NiFe-LDH sample has significantly lower overpotential than that of CDs.

The long-term stability of all samples for water oxidation is examined by measuring I–t plots at 1.23 V versus RHE (Fig. S6). All photocurrent curves decline in the beginning stage and then tend to stabilize, indicating the photogenerated holes cannot effectively travel to electrolytes in time. The photocurrent for BiVO4 and NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 are stable for 3 h and no obvious decay is observed, suggesting that the BiVO4 and NiFe-LDH are stable under illumination and electrical bias. The CDs/BiVO4 shows a marked decrease in photocurrent after 3 h testing. And the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoelectrode with highest photocurrent presents a mild decline in current. These could be caused by the oxidation and sacrificing of CDs or loss of CDs during the reaction. In order to clarify the problem, the faraday efficiency of all photoanodes is further characterized. The average faradaic efficiency values are evaluated to be 98.6%, 87.3%, 97.3% and 96.1% for BiVO4, CDs/BiVO4, NiFe-LDH/BiVO4, CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4, respectively (Fig. S7). These results demonstrate that the photocurrents come from the light decomposition water rather than oxidation and sacrificing of carbon dots.

Theoretically, the photocurrent density (Jph ) is governed by the relation: Jph = Jabs × ηseparation × ηinjection , where Jabs is the photocurrent density at 100% internal quantum efficiency; ηseparation is the photogenerated charge separation efficiency for producing surface-reaching holes; and ηinjection is charge injection efficiency of surface-reaching holes participating in desired electrochemical reactions. Previous studies have shown that Na2SO3 can be used as a hole scavenger to harvest photoexctied holes with nearly 100% efficiency [5]. To decouple the PEC properties, the charge separation efficiency and the charge injection efficiency are seperated by comparing the photocurrent density deriving from oxidizing H2O, Na2SO3 and Jabs (ηinjection = JH2
O/JNa2
SO3 , ηseparation = JNa2
SO3 /Jabs ). In order to calculate the Jabs of pristine BiVO4 and CDs/BiVO4, light harvesting efficiency (LHE) was calculated from the absorbance data shown in Fig. 4 a (LHE = (1–10−A) × 100%, A represents absorbance). As presented in Fig. 4b, the Jabs can be estimated by integrating electron flux above the bandgap energy of BiVO4 (300–500 nm). Jabs (i.e., the maximum achievable electron flux at the photoanodes) were calculated to be 5.43, and 5.47 mA/cm2 for the pristine BiVO4 and CDs/BiVO4 photoanode, respectively. The black line in Fig. 4b presents electron flux of AM 1.5G solar spectrum. The achieved charge separation efficiency and the charge injection efficiency are showed in Fig. 4c and d. After the incorporation of CDs in BiVO4, The charge separation efficiency (66.7% at 1.23 V) has a noticeable increase comparing with that of pristine BiVO4 (51.5%). But when CDs/NiFe-LDH and BiVO4 were combined, the charge seperation efficiency did not improve significantly (56.6% at 1.23 V). This indicates that no obviouschange in the bulk charge separation after the incorporation of CDs on the surface of NiFe-LDH/BiVO4. This could be caused by the poor charge mobility at the photoelectrode/OECs interface, which is thekeyinfluencefactor for the charge separation process.[6] As to the charge injection efficiency, a remarkably improvement is observed (69.2% for NiFe-LDH/BiVO4, 41.3% for CDs/BiVO4 and 28.0% for BiVO4) when incorporation of NiFe-LDH or CDs in BiVO4, implying they can improve the catalytic activity of BiVO4. The charge injection efficiency can be further increased after compositing CDs on NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 (from 69.2% to 92.8% at 1.23 V), which could be mostly because of the reduced the anodic overpotential for oxygen evolution and enhanced charge transport kinetics. Under the synergistic effect of NiFe-LDH and CDs, the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 ternary photoelectrode presents outstanding charge injection performance.

To shed light on the working mechanism of the ternary CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanodes, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to study charge transfer among the components in CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanodes. For pristine BiVO4, the O 1 s spectrum is divided into three peaks at 529.8, 531.8 and 532.9 eV (Fig. 5 a), corresponding to the lattice oxygen of BiVO4 (BiO bond),[20] OH− species[21] and adsorbed water [22], respectively. The Bi3+ state is determined by the binding energies of 159.2 and 164.6 eV for Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 (Fig. 5b) [23]. After the incorporation of CDs/NiFe-LDH, these two Bi 4f peaks decrease to 159.0 eV and 164.3 eV (Fig. 5b). For pristine NiFe-LDH, The binding energy peak of Ni 2p3/2 and Fe 2p3/2 is located at 855.7 eV and 711.2 eV, respectively [6]. A positive shift of the Ni 2p3/2 and Fe 2p3/2 peak (856.0 eV and 711.5 eV) is observed in the CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode (Fig. 5c and d), implying the + 2 and + 3 states of Ni and Fe [24]. The negative shift of the binding energies for Bi 4f along with the positive shift of both the Fe 2p and Ni 2p binding energies indicates the surface binding interaction between BiVO4 and CDs/NiFe-LDH, inducing an electron migration from NiFe-LDH to BiVO4 [6]. As for C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 5e), the binding energies present a slightly negative shift after the combination of CDs and BiVO4, indicating a rise in the electron density of CDs and thus electron migration from BiVO4 to CDs [6]. This may be attributed to the fast charge transfer and charge storage properties of CDs [7].

Based on the above analysis, a possible mechanism of the ternary CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode with increased PEC activities is presented in Fig. 5f. Above all, BiVO4 can produce an electron–hole pairs under incident light irradiation. Based on the XPS analysis (Fig. 5), the chemical binding interaction between these components induce both electron transfer from NiFe-LDH to BiVO4 and hole capture by NiFe-LDH. Then, NiFe-LDH could serve as electrocatalyst to accelerate the OER reaction. Thepresenceof CDs will additionally reduce the charge transfer resistance and the overpotentialforoxygen evolution. The rapid migration of carriers inhibits the electron–hole recombination and enhances the charge injection efficiency. As a result, under the synergetic effect of NiFe-LDH and CDs, the ternary heterostructure presents efficient PEC water splitting efficiency.

In summary, a ternary CDs/NiFe-LDH/BiVO4 photoanode with a hierarchical heterostructure has been successfully synthesized via a gradually combination of NiFe-LDH and CDs onto BiVO4 photoanode. The resulting ternary materials exhibit significantly enhanced photocurrent and charge injection efficiency. A systematic analysis confirmed that the synergistic effect between CDs and NiFe-LDH canboost up charge transport kinetics and reduce the anodic overpotential for oxygen evolution of the photoanodes. These results verify that CDs/NiFe-LDH-based ternary heterostructure designed in this work is a promising strategy for increasing PEC efficiency of photoanode. Therefore, this effective strategy could be extended to fundamentally other semiconductor photoelectrode materials (e.g. WO3, Fe2O3, etc.) for enhancing PEC water splitting performance.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported from the NSFC Major International (Regional) Joint Research Project NSFC-SNSF (51661135023), NSFC (21673091), the 973 Program of China (2014CB643506), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (HUST: 2016YXMS031), the Director Fund of the WNLO, and the Open Funds of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry (SKLEAC201607). We also thank the Analytical and Testing Centre of HUST and the Center of Micro-Fabrication and Characterization of WNLO for the sample characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.12.182.
